title: Critical Appraisal
subtitle: Academic Surgery — RCTs, Observational Studies, Diagnostics, Economic Evaluations & Clinical Guidelines
guidelines_version: CASP Checklists 2023 · Cochrane RoB 2.0 · ROBINS-I 2016 · QUADAS-2 2011 · CHEERS 2022 · AGREE II 2010 · GRADE 2013
textbook_refs: Greenhalgh T. How to Read a Paper: The Basics of Evidence-Based Medicine, 6th ed. Wiley-Blackwell, 2019
Sterne JAC et al. RoB 2: a revised tool for assessing risk of bias in randomised trials. BMJ 2019
Sterne JA et al. ROBINS-I: a tool for assessing risk of bias in non-randomised studies of interventions. BMJ 2016
Whiting PF et al. QUADAS-2: a revised tool for the quality assessment of diagnostic accuracy studies. Ann Intern Med 2011
Husereau D et al. Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards (CHEERS) 2022 Explanation and Elaboration. Value in Health 2022
Brouwers MC et al. AGREE II: advancing guideline development, reporting and evaluation in health care. CMAJ 2010
learning_objectives: Apply the CASP RCT checklist to assess validity, results, and applicability of a surgical trial
Use the Cochrane RoB 2.0 tool to assess risk of bias across 5 domains in a randomised trial
Apply ROBINS-I to assess bias in non-randomised intervention studies
Apply QUADAS-2 to appraise diagnostic accuracy studies across 4 domains
Use CHEERS 2022 to evaluate health economic evaluations in surgical oncology
Appraise qualitative research using the CASP qualitative checklist
Apply the AGREE II instrument to evaluate clinical practice guideline quality
Apply GRADE to rate evidence certainty and recommendation strength
Distinguish statistical significance from clinical significance using ARR, NNT, and effect size
Apply evidence-based surgery principles to individual patient clinical decisions

blocks.content: Critical appraisal is the systematic process of evaluating a published study to determine: (1) whether the results are valid (internal validity — was the study done correctly?); (2) what the results actually show (clinical magnitude and precision); (3) whether the results are applicable to your patient (external validity). The three-question framework — **Valid? Results? Applicable?** — underpins all critical appraisal tools including CASP, Cochrane RoB, and GRADE. Applying these three questions in sequence prevents the common error of accepting statistically significant results without interrogating whether the study was well-conducted or relevant to the clinical question.

blocks.title: CASP RCT Checklist — 11 Questions in 3 Sections
rows: Section A: Valid?
Did the trial address a clear question? Was assignment to treatment randomised?
Section A: Valid?
Were patients blinded? Were groups similar at start? Were all participants accounted for at the end?
Section B: Results?
How large was the treatment effect (ARR, RRR, NNT, HR)? How precise (95% CI)?
Section C: Applicable?
Can these results be applied to local population? Were all important outcomes considered? Are benefits worth harms and costs?

blocks.title: Board Pearl — ARR, RRR, and NNT
blocks.content: Relative risk reduction (RRR) always sounds more impressive than absolute risk reduction (ARR). Example: an intervention reduces mortality from 2% to 1% — RRR = 50% (impressive!), ARR = 1% (modest), NNT = 100 (you treat 100 patients to prevent 1 death). Always calculate and present the ARR and NNT alongside RRR. The NNT (Number Needed to Treat) = 1/ARR — it contextualises the absolute benefit and enables comparison of different interventions. A NNT of 4 for antibiotics in uncomplicated appendicitis vs a NNT of 100 for an expensive oncological targeted therapy have very different cost-effectiveness implications.

**Cochrane RoB 2.0** assesses risk of bias in RCTs across 5 domains: (1) Bias arising from the randomisation process (sequence generation + allocation concealment); (2) Bias due to deviations from intended interventions (blinding of participants + personnel); (3) Bias due to missing outcome data (completeness of follow-up); (4) Bias in measurement of the outcome (blinding of outcome assessors); (5) Bias in selection of the reported result (selective outcome reporting — compare protocol vs publication). Each domain is rated Low, Some Concerns, or High. The overall trial RoB is determined by the highest-rated domain. **ROBINS-I** (Risk Of Bias in Non-Randomised Studies of Interventions) applies 7 domains to observational studies, with pre-intervention, at-intervention, and post-intervention domains.

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Selective Outcome Reporting Bias
blocks.content: Selective outcome reporting bias occurs when a trial reports only the outcomes that showed a statistically significant result, while omitting or de-emphasising outcomes that did not. Detection: compare the trial's published report against its pre-registered protocol (ClinicalTrials.gov, ISRCTN, or PROSPERO for systematic reviews). Outcomes changed from primary to secondary, outcomes omitted from the publication, or outcomes listed in the protocol not mentioned in the paper are all red flags. The COMPare project (CEBM, 2016) found that in 67 consecutive RCTs in 5 top journals, 354 pre-specified outcomes were omitted and 357 new (non-pre-specified) outcomes were added.

blocks.title: ROBINS-I Domains for Observational Studies
rows: Confounding
Pre-intervention
Systematic differences in prognostic factors between groups
Laparoscopic cases selected for lower-risk patients
Selection of participants
Pre-intervention
Selection into the study based on characteristics related to the outcome
Including only patients who survived to follow-up
Classification of interventions
At intervention
Misclassification of exposure or comparison
Inconsistent definition of 'laparoscopic' (pure vs hand-assisted)
Deviations from intended interventions
Post-intervention
Unintended co-interventions or contamination
Open group receiving ERAS protocol not used at laparoscopic time period
Missing data
Post-intervention
Exclusion of participants with missing outcomes
Losses to follow-up systematically different between groups
Measurement of outcomes
Post-intervention
Differential assessment of outcomes between groups
Surgeons reviewing complications of their own procedures
Selection of reported results
Post-intervention
Selective reporting of outcomes
Only reporting outcomes that favour the intervention of interest

blocks.title: External Validity — Questions for Surgical Trial Applicability
rows: Patient population
Do my patients resemble the trial population?
Trials often exclude elderly, obese, comorbid patients most common in practice
Surgeon expertise
Was the trial conducted in high-volume specialist centres?
Results may not transfer to lower-volume settings (volume-outcome relationship)
Learning curve
Was the experimental technique new at trial onset?
CLASSIC trial: higher conversion rate early in learning curve
Co-interventions
Did trial patients receive systematic co-interventions (ERAS) not standard locally?
Co-interventions can drive outcome differences, not the experimental treatment alone
Outcome definition
Are the trial's outcome definitions consistent with local reporting?
Anastomotic leak definitions vary — ISREC vs clinical leak vs radiological leak
Follow-up duration
Is the follow-up long enough for the outcome of interest?
5-year survival inadequate for adjuvant therapy trials in early-stage cancer

blocks.title: Board Pearl — The Volume-Outcome Relationship
blocks.content: Many surgical procedures show a robust volume-outcome relationship: higher-volume surgeons and hospitals have lower mortality and morbidity for complex procedures (oesophagectomy, pancreatectomy, CRS+HIPEC). Birkmeyer JD et al. (NEJM 2002) demonstrated that surgeons in the highest volume quintile for pancreatectomy had mortality rates of 3.8% vs 14.3% in the lowest quintile. When applying surgical trial results to your setting, ask: were the trial surgeons operating at a volume comparable to your institution? If trials were conducted exclusively in expert high-volume centres, results may not generalise to lower-volume settings.

Diagnostic accuracy studies evaluate how well a test (index test) identifies patients with or without a target condition compared to a reference standard. Critical appraisal of diagnostic studies requires specific tools because the biases that affect diagnostic accuracy studies differ fundamentally from those affecting therapeutic trials. QUADAS-2 (Quality Assessment of Diagnostic Accuracy Studies — version 2) is the current standard tool, structured around 4 domains and 2 assessment questions per domain (risk of bias and concerns about applicability). QUADAS-2 is required by the Cochrane Diagnostic Test Accuracy Working Group and recommended by PRISMA-DTA for systematic reviews of diagnostic accuracy.

blocks.title: QUADAS-2 — Four Domains and Key Signalling Questions
rows: Patient selection
Was the patient sample representative of the clinical question?
Was a consecutive or random sample enrolled? Was a case-control design avoided?
Spectrum bias: including only clear-cut positives and obvious negatives inflates apparent diagnostic accuracy
Index test
Was the index test interpreted without knowledge of the reference standard?
Were index test results interpreted blinded to reference standard? Was a threshold pre-specified?
Incorporation bias; review bias when index test is part of reference standard assessment
Reference standard
Was the reference standard likely to correctly classify the target condition?
Is the reference standard likely to classify the target condition correctly? Were reference standard results blinded to index test?
Imperfect reference standard (e.g., imaging as reference for histological diagnosis)
Flow and timing
Was the timing appropriate and all patients included in analysis?
Was the interval between index test and reference standard appropriate? Were all patients included in the analysis?
Partial verification bias (only index test positives undergo reference standard); differential verification bias (different reference standards for positives and negatives)

blocks.content: **Spectrum bias** is the most clinically important bias in diagnostic accuracy studies and is frequently encountered in surgical literature. It occurs when the spectrum of disease severity in the study population does not match the clinical population in which the test will be used. A study evaluating CT-PET for colorectal peritoneal metastases that includes only patients with grossly positive imaging vs clearly disease-free controls will produce artificially high sensitivity and specificity — the test performs best at the extremes of disease severity, not in the clinically challenging middle ground where diagnostic discrimination is actually needed. When appraising a diagnostic study, ask: does the study population include the full spectrum of patients who would undergo this test in practice — including those with equivocal findings?

blocks.title: Clinical Application — Appraising PET-CT for Peritoneal Metastases Detection
blocks.content: A study reports PET-CT sensitivity 89%, specificity 94% for detecting peritoneal metastases in colorectal cancer patients being assessed for CRS+HIPEC candidacy. QUADAS-2 appraisal reveals: **Patient selection** — study enrolled patients referred specifically for tertiary centre assessment (spectrum bias: highest index suspicion); consecutive enrolment from a prospective database (low selection bias risk). **Index test** — PET-CT reads performed blinded to laparoscopic findings (low bias risk). **Reference standard** — diagnostic laparoscopy as reference standard (appropriate; high sensitivity for peritoneal disease — reference standard quality High). **Flow** — 8% of patients did not undergo reference standard laparoscopy (lost to follow-up); unclear if this is informative censoring. Applicability concern: the study population (tertiary referral patients suspected of peritoneal disease) may have higher prevalence than a general colorectal cancer surveillance population — positive predictive value will be inflated in lower-prevalence settings.

**Partial verification bias** (also called work-up bias) occurs when only a subset of patients who undergo the index test also receive the reference standard — specifically, when the decision to undergo the reference standard is influenced by the index test result. Example: patients with a positive CT-scan for peritoneal metastases all undergo diagnostic laparoscopy (reference standard), but patients with a negative CT do not — because laparoscopy is considered unnecessary. The true-negative rate among CT-negative patients is never assessed, artificially inflating apparent specificity. **Differential verification bias** is a related problem occurring when different subgroups receive different reference standards (e.g., CT-positive patients get laparoscopy; CT-negative patients get surveillance imaging), making cross-group comparison invalid.

Health economic evaluations quantify the relationship between resources invested (costs) and health outcomes achieved, enabling comparison of competing interventions on grounds of efficiency rather than effectiveness alone. Four types of economic evaluation exist: cost-effectiveness analysis (CEA — outcomes in natural units such as life-years or events avoided); cost-utility analysis (CUA — outcomes in quality-adjusted life-years, QALYs — most widely used for healthcare decision-making); cost-benefit analysis (CBA — outcomes monetised, enabling comparison across health and non-health sectors); and cost-minimisation analysis (CMA — outcomes assumed equivalent; cheapest option identified).

blocks.title: CHEERS 2022 — Key Domains for Appraising Economic Evaluations
rows: Title
Is this clearly identified as a health economic evaluation?
Study type not stated in title
Perspective
Whose costs are included? Healthcare payer? Society? Hospital?
Perspective not stated — different perspectives include different costs
Time horizon
Is the time horizon sufficient to capture all relevant costs and outcomes?
5-year horizon for cancer surgery misses late recurrences and cure fractions
Discount rate
Are future costs and outcomes discounted, and at what rate?
No discounting of future outcomes; discount rate not justified
Comparators
Are comparators clinically relevant and representing current standard of care?
Comparison to suboptimal or obsolete standard care inflates economic efficiency
Effect inputs
What is the source of effectiveness data? RCT? Observational cohort?
Effectiveness from single observational study rather than systematic review
ICER
Is the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio reported with uncertainty?
ICER reported without confidence interval or probabilistic sensitivity analysis
Sensitivity analysis
Are deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses performed?
Only one-way deterministic SA — probabilistic SA (PSA) missing
Equity
Are distributional effects considered?
Ignored — CRS+HIPEC is resource-intensive and access is inequitable

blocks.title: ICER — Incremental Cost-Effectiveness Ratio
blocks.content: The ICER is the key output of a cost-effectiveness analysis: **ICER = (Cost of A − Cost of B) / (QALY of A − QALY of B)**. The ICER represents the additional cost required to gain one additional QALY with intervention A compared to B. ICER thresholds vary by country: UK (NICE): £20,000–£30,000/QALY (interventions below £20k are cost-effective; those above £30k require additional justification); Spain (Spanish Agency of Medicines and Medical Devices, AEMPS guideline): €30,000/QALY considered a common threshold although not officially legislated; USA: no official threshold, but analyses commonly use $50,000–$150,000/QALY. An ICER below threshold = cost-effective (more health per £/€ spent); above threshold = not cost-effective in standard interpretation. Note: the ICER threshold is not a hard clinical recommendation threshold — context, equity, and innovation considerations apply.

**Sensitivity analysis** is mandatory in health economic evaluations because model parameters are uncertain. Two types: (1) **Deterministic (one-way or multi-way)** — varies one parameter at a time across its plausible range to assess impact on the ICER; results presented as tornado diagram (parameters with greatest impact at the top); (2) **Probabilistic sensitivity analysis (PSA)** — simultaneously varies all uncertain parameters according to probability distributions (e.g., survival probability ~ Beta distribution; costs ~ Gamma distribution), runs 10,000 simulations, and produces a cost-effectiveness acceptability curve (CEAC) showing the probability of cost-effectiveness at each willingness-to-pay threshold. PSA is the gold standard for communicating ICER uncertainty and is required by NICE, AEMPS, and most health technology appraisal bodies.

Qualitative research investigates phenomena that cannot be reduced to numerical measurements — patient experiences, clinician decision-making processes, healthcare system barriers, and the social context of surgical care. In surgical oncology, qualitative research is increasingly used to understand: patient quality of life after CRS+HIPEC (beyond validated questionnaire scores); patient decision-making about high-risk surgery; barriers to implementing enhanced recovery pathways; and the psychological impact of stoma formation. Qualitative evidence is not inferior to quantitative evidence — it addresses different, complementary research questions.

blocks.title: CASP Qualitative Checklist — 10 Questions
rows: Section A: Valid?
Q1
Was there a clear statement of the aims of the research?
Section A: Valid?
Q2
Is a qualitative methodology appropriate?
Section A: Valid?
Q3
Was the research design appropriate to address the aims?
Section A: Valid?
Q4
Was the recruitment strategy appropriate to the aims of the research?
Section A: Valid?
Q5
Was the data collected in a way that addressed the research issue?
Section A: Valid?
Q6
Has the relationship between researcher and participants been adequately considered? (Reflexivity)
Section B: Results?
Q7
Have ethical issues been taken into consideration?
Section B: Results?
Q8
Was the data analysis sufficiently rigorous?
Section B: Results?
Q9
Is there a clear statement of findings?
Section C: Applicable?
How valuable is the research?

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Reflexivity in Qualitative Research
blocks.content: **Reflexivity** (CASP question 6) refers to the explicit acknowledgement by the qualitative researcher of how their own background, beliefs, and position influenced the research — from study design through data analysis. A surgeon conducting qualitative interviews about patients' decisions to accept CRS+HIPEC must acknowledge that their position as the operating surgeon may influence how patients respond (social desirability bias), how they frame questions, and how they interpret findings. Qualitative research lacking any reflexivity statement is a significant quality concern — it does not mean the findings are invalid, but it limits the reader's ability to assess potential influence of the researcher's perspective on the interpretive conclusions.

sections.title: Clinical Practice Guidelines — Development & Appraisal (AGREE II)

blocks.content: Clinical practice guidelines (CPGs) are systematically developed statements to assist practitioner and patient decisions about appropriate care for specific clinical circumstances. High-quality guidelines are developed using systematic evidence review, expert consensus, stakeholder involvement, and explicit processes for translating evidence into recommendations. The quality of published guidelines varies enormously — from rigorous GRADE-informed documents (ESMO, NICE, ASCO) to expert-opinion-based statements lacking systematic evidence review. The AGREE II (Appraisal of Guidelines Research and Evaluation) instrument enables structured assessment of guideline quality across 6 domains.

blocks.title: AGREE II — Six Domains and Scored Items
rows: Scope and purpose
Overall objective, clinical questions, target population
Clinical questions formulated as PICO; target population specified
Stakeholder involvement
Guideline development group, patient views, target users
Patient/public representation in development group; piloted with target users
Rigour of development
Search strategy, evidence selection, evidence-to-recommendation, peer review, update
Systematic search documented; GRADE or equivalent used; external review conducted
Clarity of presentation
Specific recommendations, options, key recommendations
Recommendations clearly stated; different options for different patients identified
Applicability
Facilitators/barriers, advice for implementation, resource implications, monitoring criteria
Cost implications discussed; audit criteria provided
Editorial independence
Funding body, competing interests recorded
Competing interests of all panel members disclosed; funding body had no influence

blocks.content: **GRADE recommendation notation** in high-quality CPGs: recommendations are expressed as strong (⊕⊕⊕⊕) or weak/conditional (⊕⊕⊕○), and are described in terms of certainty of evidence (High/Moderate/Low/Very Low). The Evidence to Decision (EtD) framework (GRADEpro) documents the panel's reasoning for translating evidence into recommendations, considering: desirable and undesirable effects; certainty of evidence; values and preferences; balance of effects; resource requirements; health equity; acceptability; and feasibility. A guideline without GRADE evidence tables or equivalent evidence-to-recommendation documentation cannot be considered a rigorous evidence-based guideline — though it may still provide valuable expert consensus.

blocks.title: AGREE II — Rigour of Development Domain (Domain 3)
blocks.content: Domain 3 of AGREE II is the most critical for evidence-based medicine — it evaluates whether the guideline followed a rigorous process for finding and grading evidence. Key items: Item 7 (systematic methods used to search for evidence — databases, date range, search strategy documented); Item 8 (criteria for selecting evidence are clearly described); Item 10 (methods for formulating the recommendations are clearly described, including GRADE or equivalent); Item 12 (explicit link between recommendations and supporting evidence); Item 13 (guideline externally reviewed by experts prior to publication); Item 14 (procedure for updating the guideline is provided). A guideline scoring poorly on Domain 3 should be considered expert consensus rather than evidence-based guidance, and recommendations should be interpreted accordingly.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Guideline Adaptation
blocks.content: Guideline adaptation refers to the systematic process of adopting or modifying a high-quality guideline developed in one context for use in a different context (different country, healthcare system, or patient population). The ADAPTE methodology provides a structured approach: (1) assess the source guideline's quality using AGREE II; (2) determine whether the clinical context differs sufficiently to require modification; (3) identify recommendations that require adaptation vs those that can be adopted as-is; (4) conduct local stakeholder review and piloting; (5) establish a monitoring and update process. In Spain, major oncological guidelines (e.g., ESMO colorectal cancer guidelines) are adapted to the Spanish context by SEOM (Sociedad Española de Oncología Médica) and AEC (Asociación Española de Cirujanos), accounting for medication availability, healthcare system structure, and local epidemiology.

sections.title: Evidence-Based Surgery — Applying Evidence at the Bedside

blocks.content: Evidence-based medicine (EBM) in surgery is not about applying clinical trial results mechanically to every patient — it is the integration of: (1) **Current best evidence** (systematic reviews, RCTs, observational studies, appropriately weighted by quality); (2) **Clinical expertise** (surgeon's experiential knowledge of likely individual outcomes and risks); (3) **Patient values and preferences** (what matters to this specific patient, given full information about options, risks, and likely outcomes). The tension between EBM and surgical practice arises because: RCTs exclude many real patients; surgical expertise varies enormously between centres; and patient values are heterogeneous. Applying trial evidence to the individual requires bridging all three.

blocks.title: EBM at the Bedside — Five Steps for Surgical Decisions
rows: 1. Ask
Convert clinical uncertainty into an answerable PICO question
PICO framework; background vs foreground questions
2. Acquire
Search for the best available evidence efficiently
PubMed Clinical Queries; Cochrane Library; UpToDate for synthesis
3. Appraise
Critically evaluate study validity, results, and applicability
CASP checklists; RoB 2.0; GRADE summary tables
4. Apply
Integrate evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
Shared decision-making; patient decision aids; risk communication
5. Audit
Evaluate your own patient outcomes against evidence benchmarks
Local quality audit; morbidity-mortality meeting; NACR/BSGD registry comparison

blocks.title: Clinical Application — Applying CRS+HIPEC Evidence to an Individual Patient
blocks.content:
A 58-year-old woman with colorectal peritoneal metastases (PCI 14, CRS achievable, no systemic metastases, PS 1, CEA 8 ng/mL) is referred for CRS+HIPEC consideration. EBM at the bedside:

**Evidence:** PRODIGE 7 (Quénet, ASCO 2018/Lancet Oncology 2021) — no OS benefit for HIPEC vs CRS alone (OS 41.7 vs 41.2 months, HR 1.00); but this was with short intraperitoneal oxaliplatin exposure. UNICANCER PRODIGE 7 trial population PCI 7 median — PCI 14 is higher-risk. PSOGI retrospective data shows benefit at PCI<15. GRADE certainty: Moderate (one RCT with methodological concerns).

**Clinical expertise:** Your centre has performed 120 CRS+HIPEC procedures; your anastomotic leak rate is 6%; your cytoreduction rate (CC0/1) for PCI 14 is 92%. These metrics are consistent with high-volume centres from which the evidence derives.

**Patient values:** Patient states she understands the risk (9% major morbidity) but 'would rather try everything possible given a potential for long-term remission.' She has 3 young children and values the chance of extended survival over avoidance of morbidity.

**Decision:** CRS+HIPEC is offered. The evidence is modest but consistent with potential benefit for PCI<15; your centre metrics are appropriate; patient values align with an aggressive approach. Shared decision-making documentation should record this process.

**Risk communication** is a core EBM skill in surgical oncology. Communicating probabilities to patients effectively requires: (1) using absolute risks rather than relative risks ('1 in 10 patients' rather than '10%'); (2) providing visual aids (natural frequencies, icon arrays); (3) presenting both benefits and harms in the same format and time frame; (4) acknowledging uncertainty explicitly ('the evidence shows an average benefit, but we cannot predict your individual response'); (5) using patient decision aids when available (validated for colostomy decisions, bowel resection). The SPIKES framework (Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Emotions, Strategy) provides structure for delivering difficult probabilistic information in the surgical consultation.

consolidation.intro: Test your critical appraisal knowledge with 20 clinical questions covering RCTs, observational studies, diagnostic accuracy, health economics, qualitative research, and clinical guidelines.

questions.stem: An RCT of laparoscopic versus open right hemicolectomy reports statistically significant reduction in complications (laparoscopic 18% vs open 28%, p=0.03, NNT=10) at two academic centres with experienced laparoscopic surgeons. You work at a district general hospital performing 25 right hemicolectomies per year. Which critical appraisal concern is MOST relevant to applying these results?

options.A: The p-value of 0.03 is close to the significance threshold and should not be trusted
options.B: The trial is too small to detect a clinically meaningful difference
options.C: The results may not be applicable to a lower-volume district general setting — volume-outcome and expertise effects limit external validity
options.D: Blinding was not achieved — surgeons knew which operation they performed, invalidating the results

explanation.correct: The MOST relevant concern for applying these results is external validity (applicability). The trial was conducted in high-volume academic centres with experienced laparoscopic surgeons — a setting fundamentally different from a district general hospital performing 25 cases/year. The volume-outcome relationship is well-established for colorectal cancer surgery. The NNT of 10 may be accurate for expert centres but could be very different in lower-volume settings. External validity is the primary concern when well-conducted trial results come from a setting that doesn't match your practice.

wrong.A: A p-value of 0.03 is a legitimate result — the concern is not the p-value proximity but whether the result applies to your setting.
wrong.B: With a 10% absolute difference detected and p=0.03, the trial was adequately powered — underpowering applies when there is a non-significant result.
wrong.D: In surgical trials, surgeon blinding is impossible — this is a universal limitation. The relevant question is outcome assessor blinding.

explanation.guideline: CASP RCT Checklist 2023 — Section C (Applicability): 'Is your setting so different from the trial setting that the methods could not be used there?'
explanation.trial: Birkmeyer JD et al. (NEJM 2002): mortality rates varied 4-fold between highest and lowest volume centres for colectomy.
explanation.pearl: External validity is often the most clinically relevant critical appraisal issue in surgery — ask 'Can I do this operation as well as the trial surgeons?' before applying trial results to your patients.

questions.stem: QUADAS-2 is being applied to a study evaluating CT-scan diagnostic accuracy for peritoneal metastases in colorectal cancer. The study enrolled patients referred to a tertiary HIPEC centre specifically because imaging showed suspected peritoneal disease. Patients with clearly negative CT scans were not enrolled because they did not proceed to laparoscopy (the reference standard). Which bias domain of QUADAS-2 is MOST affected?

options.A: Index test domain — the CT scan was interpreted with knowledge of clinical suspicion
options.B: Patient selection domain — spectrum bias and partial verification bias both operate through patient selection
options.C: Reference standard domain — laparoscopy is an imperfect reference standard for peritoneal metastases
options.D: Flow and timing domain — delayed reference standard assessment introduces information bias

explanation.correct: Two biases in the patient selection domain are operating simultaneously: (1) Spectrum bias — enrolling only patients already suspected of peritoneal disease (highest pre-test probability) means the study population does not represent the full clinical spectrum in which CT would be used (from clearly negative to clearly positive); CT performs best at extremes of disease severity, inflating sensitivity and specificity. (2) Partial verification bias — only patients with positive CT (who then proceeded to laparoscopy) have their diagnoses confirmed by the reference standard; CT-negative patients without laparoscopy contribute no true-negative data, inflating apparent specificity. Both biases arise in the patient selection domain of QUADAS-2.

wrong.A: Index test domain covers interpretation bias — the question specifically describes patient selection and reference standard access, not CT interpretation.
wrong.C: Laparoscopy is actually a high-quality reference standard for peritoneal metastases — this is a strength of the study, not a domain concern.
wrong.D: Flow and timing concerns delayed assessment — the question describes patient selection and verification bias, not timing.

explanation.guideline: QUADAS-2 Domain 1 (Patient selection) — spectrum bias and partial verification bias are both classified under patient selection domain
explanation.trial: Whiting PF et al. QUADAS-2: a revised tool for the quality assessment of diagnostic accuracy studies. Ann Intern Med 2011.
explanation.pearl: Spectrum bias and partial verification bias are the two most prevalent and impactful biases in surgical diagnostic accuracy studies — both operate through patient selection. Always ask: (1) Is the spectrum of disease in the study representative of clinical practice? (2) Did all patients receive the reference standard, or only those with positive index tests?

questions.stem: A health economic analysis reports that CRS+HIPEC has an ICER of €28,000/QALY compared to systemic chemotherapy alone for colorectal peritoneal metastases, from the Spanish National Health System perspective over a 10-year time horizon. Sensitivity analysis shows the ICER ranges from €18,000 to €52,000/QALY. What is the most appropriate interpretation?

options.A: CRS+HIPEC is definitively cost-effective — the base-case ICER of €28,000 is below the €30,000/QALY threshold
options.B: CRS+HIPEC may be cost-effective at the base-case estimate, but the sensitivity analysis range crosses the €30,000 threshold — the result is uncertain and depends strongly on key model parameters
options.C: CRS+HIPEC is not cost-effective — the ICER range includes values above €30,000
options.D: The ICER is only valid from the societal perspective — the NHS perspective underestimates true costs

explanation.correct: The base-case ICER of €28,000/QALY is below the commonly used €30,000 threshold for Spain, suggesting cost-effectiveness in the central estimate. However, the sensitivity analysis shows the ICER ranges from €18,000 to €52,000 — the upper bound substantially exceeds €30,000. This means that under plausible alternative parameter assumptions (different survival estimates, different morbidity costs, different QALY utilities), CRS+HIPEC may not be cost-effective. The correct interpretation acknowledges cost-effectiveness in the base case while explicitly stating that the result is sensitive to key parameters and that probabilistic sensitivity analysis (CEAC) is needed to quantify the probability of cost-effectiveness at the €30,000 threshold.

wrong.A: A base-case estimate below threshold does not guarantee cost-effectiveness when sensitivity analysis shows the ICER can exceed threshold — uncertainty must be communicated.
wrong.C: A range crossing the threshold does not automatically make an intervention not cost-effective — the probability of being below threshold matters, not just whether the range is contained.
wrong.D: Healthcare payer (NHS) perspective is entirely legitimate — societal perspective is broader and may be used for some decisions, but NHS perspective is the standard for healthcare resource allocation decisions in Spain.

explanation.guideline: CHEERS 2022 item 18 — probabilistic sensitivity analysis required to characterise uncertainty; sensitivity analysis results must inform interpretation of ICER
explanation.trial: Franko J et al. Colorectal peritoneal metastases treated with systemic chemotherapy and cytoreductive surgery with HIPEC: a systematic review. Ann Surg Oncol 2010.
explanation.pearl: ICER interpretation requires: (1) compare to the jurisdiction-specific WTP threshold; (2) examine sensitivity analysis range; (3) review the CEAC (cost-effectiveness acceptability curve) — this shows probability of cost-effectiveness at each WTP threshold, providing the most comprehensive uncertainty characterisation.

questions.stem: Cochrane RoB 2.0 is applied to a surgical RCT comparing mesh vs no-mesh inguinal hernia repair. Domain 2 (deviations from intended interventions) is rated 'High' risk. What is the most likely reason and what is the consequence for the overall trial risk of bias rating?

options.A: High risk in Domain 2 indicates allocation concealment failure — the overall trial is rated High risk of bias
options.B: Domain 2 'High' most likely reflects that participants and/or surgeons were not blinded to the intervention — because surgical blinding is impossible; however, if the outcome (pain, recurrence) is objectively assessed by blinded assessors, Domain 2 alone may not invalidate the trial
options.C: High risk in Domain 2 mandates retraction of the trial
options.D: Domain 2 rating affects only secondary outcomes — primary outcomes are rated by Domain 1 only

explanation.correct: In surgical RCTs, Domain 2 of RoB 2.0 (deviations from intended interventions) typically receives the most scrutiny because surgical blinding of participants and surgeons is impossible by the nature of the intervention. RoB 2.0 distinguishes between the 'effect of assignment to intervention' (intention-to-treat analysis, appropriate for pragmatic questions) and 'effect of adhering to intervention' (per-protocol analysis, appropriate for explanatory questions). The overall trial risk of bias is determined by the highest-rated domain — Domain 2 'High' would rate the overall trial as 'High' risk. However, if blinded outcome assessment was used (e.g., radiological recurrence assessed by a radiologist blinded to operative group), Domain 4 (measurement of outcome) may be Low, partly mitigating the Domain 2 concern in the assessment of objective outcomes. For subjective outcomes (pain) without assessor blinding, Domain 4 would also be High.

wrong.A: Allocation concealment failure is Domain 1 (randomisation process), not Domain 2. Domains are distinct in RoB 2.0.
wrong.C: High risk of bias is a quality assessment finding — it informs interpretation of results and GRADE certainty, but does not mandate retraction.
wrong.D: RoB 2.0 is applied outcome by outcome — a trial may have different RoB ratings for different outcomes. Domain 2 affects any outcome susceptible to performance bias.

explanation.guideline: Sterne JAC et al. RoB 2: a revised tool for assessing risk of bias in randomised trials. BMJ 2019 — Domain 2 guidance for surgical trials
explanation.trial: Lundström KJ et al. Outcome of laparoscopic and open surgery for inguinal hernia — insights from the Swedish Hernia Register. Br J Surg 2012.
explanation.pearl: In surgical RCTs, Domain 2 'High' is almost universal due to impossibility of blinding — this should not automatically doom an entire trial. Focus critical appraisal on: (1) Was outcome assessment blinded? (Domain 4); (2) Was follow-up complete? (Domain 3); (3) Were protocols pre-registered? (Domain 5). These domains are within the control of surgical trialists.

questions.stem: A diagnostic accuracy study reports CT sensitivity 87% and specificity 91% for detecting anastomotic leak after anterior resection. The study enrolled 200 patients; 18 had confirmed anastomotic leaks (prevalence 9%). A patient presents with clinical suspicion for leak. Calculate the positive predictive value (PPV) at this prevalence.

options.A: PPV = 87% — equal to sensitivity
options.B: PPV = 47% — using Bayes' theorem with 9% prevalence, sensitivity 87%, specificity 91%
options.C: PPV = 91% — equal to specificity
options.D: PPV cannot be calculated without knowing the population size

explanation.correct: PPV depends on prevalence, sensitivity, and specificity — Bayes' theorem: PPV = (Sensitivity × Prevalence) / [(Sensitivity × Prevalence) + ((1-Specificity) × (1-Prevalence))]. With sensitivity=0.87, specificity=0.91, prevalence=0.09: Numerator = 0.87 × 0.09 = 0.0783. Denominator = 0.0783 + (0.09 × 0.91) = 0.0783 + 0.0819 = 0.1602. PPV = 0.0783/0.1602 ≈ 0.49 ≈ 49%. Approximately half of CT-positive results represent true anastomotic leaks at this prevalence. This counterintuitive result — despite high sensitivity and specificity, PPV is only ~49% — is explained by the low prevalence (9%). In a lower-prevalence setting (e.g., routine post-operative surveillance with 2% prevalence), PPV would be even lower (~17%), meaning most CT-positive results would be false alarms.

wrong.A: PPV is not equal to sensitivity — sensitivity describes performance in true positive patients; PPV describes performance conditional on a positive test result.
wrong.C: PPV is not equal to specificity — specificity describes performance in true negative patients.
wrong.D: PPV can be calculated from sensitivity, specificity, and prevalence alone using Bayes' theorem — population size is not required.

explanation.guideline: QUADAS-2 applicability concern — studies conducted at high prevalence settings produce artificially high PPV; PPV must be recalculated for the local prevalence
explanation.trial: Deeks JJ. Systematic reviews of evaluations of diagnostic and screening tests. BMJ 2001 — foundational paper on PPV, NPV, and prevalence effects.
explanation.pearl: Bayes' theorem for PPV: (Se × prev) / [(Se × prev) + ((1-Sp) × (1-prev))]. Always recalculate PPV and NPV for your local prevalence when applying diagnostic accuracy data from a study population with different disease prevalence.

questions.stem: A qualitative study explores patients' experiences of living with a permanent colostomy after low anterior resection was declined. Purposive sampling was used to recruit 15 patients from a stoma support group. Which critical appraisal concern does this recruitment strategy primarily raise?

options.A: The sample is too small — qualitative studies require at least 30 participants for validity
options.B: Purposive sampling from a stoma support group may over-represent patients who have adjusted well to their stoma — those with severe psychological difficulties may not attend support groups, leading to a positive skew in the experience findings
options.C: Purposive sampling is inappropriate — only random sampling is valid for qualitative research
options.D: The study should have used quantitative questionnaires instead of qualitative interviews

explanation.correct: Purposive sampling selects participants deliberately based on specific characteristics relevant to the research question — it is an appropriate and commonly used sampling strategy in qualitative research. The specific concern here is the recruitment context: stoma support group members are likely to be patients who have sought social support, are engaged with the healthcare system, and may have achieved better psychosocial adjustment. Patients experiencing severe depression, social isolation, or profound quality-of-life impairment may be less likely to attend support groups and are therefore under-represented. This is a form of sampling bias that would skew findings toward more positive experiences of colostomy. A more comprehensive sampling strategy would include recruitment from clinic settings, community networks, and specific outreach to patients who have disengaged from follow-up.

wrong.A: Qualitative sample size is not determined by a minimum number — adequacy is determined by data saturation (the point at which additional interviews yield no new themes). 15 participants may be entirely appropriate.
wrong.C: Purposive sampling is standard and appropriate in qualitative research — random sampling is unnecessary and often counterproductive when specific experiences are being sought.
wrong.D: Qualitative and quantitative methods address different questions — qualitative interviews exploring lived experience cannot be replaced by questionnaires without losing the depth of the phenomenon being studied.

explanation.guideline: CASP Qualitative Checklist Q4 — 'Was the recruitment strategy appropriate to the aims of the research?' — bias in recruitment context is a validity concern
explanation.trial: Scarpa M et al. Quality of life of disease-free rectal cancer patients. Health Qual Life Outcomes 2011.
explanation.pearl: In qualitative appraisal: purposive sampling is appropriate but must be examined for context bias. Data saturation (not a minimum n) determines sample adequacy. Reflexivity (researcher's position acknowledged) is a validity criterion unique to qualitative research.

questions.stem: An AGREE II assessment of a European surgical oncology guideline on CRS+HIPEC for colorectal peritoneal metastases scores Domain 3 (Rigour of Development) at 28/100. What does this score indicate and what is its clinical implication?

options.A: The guideline is of acceptable quality — AGREE II scores above 25 indicate adequate rigour
options.B: A Domain 3 score of 28/100 indicates poor methodological rigour in guideline development — the guideline likely lacks systematic evidence review, GRADE evidence grading, and external expert review; its recommendations should be interpreted as expert consensus rather than evidence-based guidance
options.C: Domain 3 is the least important AGREE II domain — clinical applicability (Domain 5) is more relevant
options.D: The guideline should be retracted — AGREE II scores below 50 render guidelines invalid

explanation.correct: AGREE II Domain 3 (Rigour of Development) covers 8 items (7–14) addressing the evidence base and methodology of guideline development: systematic search strategy, evidence selection criteria, strengths and limitations of evidence body, formulation methods (GRADE or equivalent), health benefits/harms/risks considered, explicit links between recommendations and evidence, external expert review, and update procedure. A score of 28/100 indicates that most of these items are poorly reported or absent — the guideline was likely developed through expert consensus without systematic evidence review or formal evidence grading. Clinical implication: the recommendations cannot be assumed to reflect the best available evidence synthesis; they may represent traditional practice, regional expert opinion, or may not account for recent RCTs that contradict established practice. Such guidelines should be used with explicit awareness of their expert-consensus basis.

wrong.A: There is no AGREE II minimum score that defines 'acceptable quality' — scores must be interpreted in context, and a score of 28/100 on the most critical methodological domain clearly indicates poor rigour.
wrong.C: Domain 3 (Rigour of Development) is universally considered the most critical domain in AGREE II — it determines whether the guideline's recommendations are based on systematic evidence review or expert opinion.
wrong.D: AGREE II does not define a threshold below which guidelines are 'invalid' — it provides structured evidence for informed decision-making about which guidelines to follow and with what confidence.

explanation.guideline: AGREE II — Brouwers MC et al. AGREE II: advancing guideline development, reporting and evaluation in health care. CMAJ 2010; Domain 3 highest scoring threshold >60% considered adequate rigour
explanation.trial: Alonso-Coello P et al. GRADE Evidence to Decision (EtD) frameworks: a systematic and transparent approach to making well-informed healthcare choices. BMJ 2016.
explanation.pearl: When a guideline has low AGREE II Domain 3 score: (1) search for existing systematic reviews or higher-quality guidelines on the same topic; (2) interpret recommendations as expert consensus; (3) weight them more heavily when they come from high-volume centres with direct clinical experience of the procedure; (4) actively seek patient values input rather than deferring to guideline authority.

questions.stem: A ROBINS-I assessment of a retrospective cohort study comparing laparoscopic vs open CRS+HIPEC rates the 'Confounding' domain as 'Serious' risk of bias. Which specific confounding concern is most likely in this context?

options.A: Confounding by age — laparoscopic patients are systematically younger
options.B: Indication bias — laparoscopic approach is selectively offered to patients with lower PCI, less extensive disease, and better performance status, making the laparoscopic group intrinsically lower-risk than the open group at baseline
options.C: Information bias — operative notes for laparoscopic cases are more detailed than open cases
options.D: Temporal confounding — laparoscopic CRS was adopted later, coinciding with improvements in perioperative care

explanation.correct: In retrospective cohort studies comparing laparoscopic vs open surgical approaches, indication bias is the most pervasive and serious confounding threat. Laparoscopic CRS is typically reserved for patients with lower disease burden (lower PCI, fewer adhesions, more limited peritoneal distribution) who are inherently better candidates for successful cytoreduction. The open group contains more extensive disease cases that were converted from laparoscopic, cases deemed too complex for minimally invasive approach, and potentially patients with poorer performance status. These baseline differences — not the surgical approach — may explain any observed outcome differences. Multivariable adjustment can partially address measured confounders (PCI, PS, CEA), but unmeasured confounders (surgical complexity within PCI categories, laparoscopic skill) remain. ROBINS-I would rate this as 'Serious' confounding if the covariates used for adjustment do not adequately capture all prognostically important selection factors.

wrong.A: Age is a measurable confounder that can be adjusted for — by itself it is unlikely to rate the domain as 'Serious' if included in multivariable analysis.
wrong.C: Information bias about operative documentation is not a confounding bias — it is a measurement bias, addressed in the 'Classification of interventions' domain of ROBINS-I.
wrong.D: Temporal confounding (calendar time bias) is a valid concern but would be classified under 'Deviations from intended interventions' or addressed separately as a time-trend confounder in multivariable analysis.

explanation.guideline: Sterne JA et al. ROBINS-I: a tool for assessing risk of bias in non-randomised studies. BMJ 2016 — Domain 1 (Confounding) guidance
explanation.trial: Cai J et al. ROBINS-I guidance — confounding examples in surgical cohort studies. J Clin Epidemiol 2019.
explanation.pearl: Indication bias is the surgical equivalent of confounding by indication — 'better patients get better operations.' It is the primary reason why observational evidence consistently overestimates laparoscopic benefit compared to RCT evidence. Propensity score matching and instrumental variable analysis are advanced methods to mitigate (but not eliminate) this bias.

questions.stem: A clinical trial reports that treatment A reduces 5-year cancer recurrence from 40% to 28% (RRR=30%, ARR=12%, NNT=8.3). The treatment requires 6 months of intravenous chemotherapy with 25% grade 3–4 toxicity and costs €45,000. How should this information be presented to support shared decision-making?

options.A: Emphasise the 30% relative risk reduction — this is the most clinically meaningful way to communicate benefit
options.B: Present ARR (12%) and NNT (8.3) alongside harms (NNH for grade 3–4 toxicity) and costs; use natural frequencies and a patient decision aid if available
options.C: Summarise as 'standard of care' — patients should defer to clinical judgment on treatment decisions
options.D: Present only the statistically significant p-value — this confirms the treatment works

explanation.correct: Shared decision-making requires transparent, balanced presentation of: (1) Absolute benefit — ARR 12% ('12 out of 100 patients treated will be prevented from having a recurrence'); (2) NNT — 'you need to treat approximately 8 patients to prevent one recurrence'; (3) Harms — NNH for grade 3–4 toxicity: if 25% of patients experience severe toxicity, NNH = 1/0.25 = 4 ('1 in 4 patients will experience severe side effects'); (4) Duration and resource implications — 6 months IV chemotherapy, €45,000 cost; (5) Patient values — is preventing recurrence worth these costs and toxicities for this specific patient? Natural frequency framing ('12 out of 100' rather than '12%') improves patient understanding and reduces misjudgement about absolute magnitude.

wrong.A: RRR is consistently found to overinflate apparent benefit in patient perception studies — presenting RRR alone (or as the primary metric) is a known communication bias that inflates patient enthusiasm for treatments that may have modest absolute benefit.
wrong.C: Deferral to clinical judgment without patient-centred information sharing is paternalistic and violates informed consent principles — patient autonomy requires that information is provided in an understandable format.
wrong.D: P-values communicate statistical significance, not clinical magnitude or relevance to the individual patient — they are not appropriate as the primary communication tool for patient decision-making.

explanation.guideline: NICE Shared Decision Making guidelines 2021; ICMJE recommendations for patient-centred communication of risk
explanation.trial: Gigerenzer G et al. Helping doctors and patients make sense of health statistics. Psychol Sci Public Interest 2007 — foundational paper on natural frequency communication of risk.
explanation.pearl: Harms-benefits communication standard: always present both NNT (benefit) and NNH (harm) in the same time frame and using the same natural frequency format. 'Treating 8 patients prevents 1 recurrence; treating 4 patients causes 1 episode of severe toxicity' is more informative and balanced than any combination of relative risks.

questions.stem: QUADAS-2 is being applied to a study of intraoperative frozen section for sentinel node biopsy in breast cancer. The reference standard is final paraffin section histology. Some patients had a negative frozen section but did not undergo completion axillary dissection — their nodal status at final histology was not available. Which QUADAS-2 domain is primarily affected?

options.A: Index test domain — frozen section was interpreted without knowledge of final histology
options.B: Reference standard domain — paraffin section is the wrong reference standard for this question
options.C: Flow and timing domain — patients with negative frozen section who did not undergo completion axillary dissection represent incomplete reference standard verification
options.D: Patient selection domain — spectrum bias in sentinel node biopsy studies

explanation.correct: The flow and timing domain of QUADAS-2 addresses whether all patients who underwent the index test also received the reference standard, and whether the timing was appropriate. In this study, patients with a negative frozen section (index test negative) did not proceed to completion axillary dissection — therefore, they did not receive the reference standard (paraffin section of axillary nodes). Their true nodal status is unknown. This is partial verification bias — the decision to perform the reference standard is determined by the index test result. Specifically, true-negative patients who were correctly identified by frozen section (and therefore spared axillary dissection) cannot be counted — creating an undercount of true negatives and artificially inflating sensitivity and specificity. This is classified under the flow domain because it relates to patient pathway through the study.

wrong.A: Index test domain addresses interpretation bias — whether frozen section was read with knowledge of paraffin results. The question describes a pathway issue (who receives the reference standard), not interpretation.
wrong.B: Paraffin section histology is an entirely appropriate and high-quality reference standard for nodal metastasis — the reference standard quality is not the concern here.
wrong.D: Patient selection domain concerns how patients were recruited into the study — the issue here occurs after recruitment, in who receives the reference standard.

explanation.guideline: QUADAS-2 Domain 4 (Flow and timing) — partial verification bias: not all patients receiving the index test also receive the reference standard
explanation.trial: Lyman GH et al. American Society of Clinical Oncology guideline recommendations for sentinel lymph node biopsy in early-stage breast cancer. J Clin Oncol 2005.
explanation.pearl: Partial verification bias in diagnostic studies: the decision to perform the reference standard should be independent of the index test result. When it is not — when positive index tests lead to reference standard and negative ones do not — specificity is inflated (false-negatives are not counted). Check the flow diagram for all patients who underwent the index test to verify complete reference standard ascertainment.

questions.stem: A health economic evaluation of robotic CRS+HIPEC versus conventional laparoscopic CRS+HIPEC uses a 2-year time horizon. The primary oncological outcome is disease-free survival at 2 years. Reviewers using CHEERS 2022 identify a critical limitation. What is it?

options.A: The comparison should be against open surgery, not laparoscopic — robotic vs laparoscopic is not a relevant comparison
options.B: A 2-year time horizon is insufficient for a cancer surgery comparison — it fails to capture late recurrences, long-term complications, and potentially cured patients who live beyond 2 years, systematically underestimating long-term survival benefits
options.C: The evaluation should have used cost-benefit analysis rather than cost-effectiveness analysis
options.D: Robotic surgery costs are too variable to model accurately — economic evaluation of robotic procedures is not methodologically valid

explanation.correct: CHEERS 2022 item 5 requires that the time horizon is 'long enough to capture all relevant future differences in costs and consequences between the comparators.' For CRS+HIPEC, a 2-year time horizon is critically insufficient because: (1) a substantial proportion of colorectal peritoneal metastases patients are alive at 2 years — whether as cured patients or with controlled disease — meaning long-term survival differences are not captured; (2) late recurrences occur beyond 2 years and represent significant subsequent treatment costs; (3) the distinction between robotic and conventional laparoscopic is primarily about short-term morbidity and cost differences — a 2-year horizon may capture sufficient perioperative outcome data but misses the lifetime implications of differing oncological outcomes. Health economic guidelines for oncology (NICE, AEMPS) recommend lifetime time horizons for curative-intent cancer surgery to capture all relevant costs and outcomes.

wrong.A: Robotic vs laparoscopic CRS is a clinically relevant comparison as minimally invasive techniques expand in peritoneal oncology — the comparator choice is not the identified critical limitation.
wrong.C: Cost-effectiveness analysis (CEA/CUA) is entirely appropriate for surgical comparisons — cost-benefit analysis (monetising health outcomes) is less commonly used in healthcare and not inherently superior.
wrong.D: Robotic surgery costs, while variable, are estimable from institutional data, manufacturer pricing, and literature — economic evaluation is methodologically valid for any intervention with quantifiable costs.

explanation.guideline: CHEERS 2022 item 5 — 'Time horizon: state the time horizon for estimating costs and consequences...and motivate its choice'
explanation.trial: Husereau D et al. Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards (CHEERS) 2022 Explanation and Elaboration. Value in Health 2022.
explanation.pearl: Lifetime time horizon rule: for any condition where a meaningful proportion of patients can be cured or where long-term sequelae are important, use a lifetime horizon (modelled survival curves extrapolated beyond trial follow-up). Shorter horizons systematically underestimate benefits for curative interventions and are a common CHEERS deficiency in surgical economic evaluations.

questions.stem: A clinical practice guideline recommends: 'CRS+HIPEC should be offered to selected patients with colorectal peritoneal metastases (STRONG recommendation, LOW certainty evidence).' Under GRADE methodology, can a strong recommendation be made on low certainty evidence?

options.A: No — GRADE prohibits strong recommendations when evidence certainty is below Moderate
options.B: Yes — recommendation strength and evidence certainty are rated independently; a strong recommendation based on low certainty evidence is appropriate when the magnitude of potential benefit substantially outweighs risk, or when inaction has catastrophic consequences
options.C: Yes — but the strong recommendation automatically upgrades the evidence certainty to Moderate
options.D: No — low certainty evidence always generates a conditional (weak) recommendation

explanation.correct: In GRADE methodology, recommendation strength and evidence certainty are determined independently. A strong recommendation requires that the panel is confident that the desirable effects of the intervention clearly outweigh the undesirable effects for the vast majority of well-informed patients — this judgment considers: magnitude of benefit, certainty of evidence, values and preferences, cost, equity, feasibility, and acceptability. In specific circumstances, strong recommendations on low certainty evidence are appropriate: (1) when the intervention offers the only potentially curative option and inaction is catastrophic (e.g., CRS+HIPEC for resectable peritoneal metastases where systemic chemotherapy alone carries median OS of 12–18 months); (2) when the benefit-risk ratio is highly favourable despite uncertainty; (3) when qualitative evidence or biological plausibility strongly supports benefit. GRADE labels such recommendations as 'strong recommendation, low certainty' and notes that future high-quality evidence could change the recommendation.

wrong.A: GRADE explicitly permits strong recommendations at any evidence certainty level — the Evidence to Decision framework documents the reasoning.
wrong.C: Recommendation strength does not affect evidence certainty rating — they are distinct assessments. Evidence certainty reflects the quality and consistency of primary studies; recommendation strength reflects the net benefit assessment.
wrong.D: Low certainty evidence generates conditional recommendations as a default, but the evidence-to-decision framework can support strong recommendations when specific criteria are met — as described in the correct answer.

explanation.guideline: GRADE Handbook — 'Strong recommendations can be made despite low certainty when the potential benefits substantially outweigh potential harms regardless of the quality of supporting evidence'
explanation.trial: Andrews JC et al. GRADE guidelines 15: going from evidence to recommendation — determinants of a recommendation's direction and strength. J Clin Epidemiol 2013.
explanation.pearl: Mnemonic for strong recommendation determinants (GRADE): **B**enefits outweigh harms; **C**ertainty of evidence (high preferred but not required); **V**alues and preferences (most patients would choose it); **R**esources (acceptable cost). All four must be considered — evidence certainty is one of four, not the sole determinant.

questions.stem: A prospective study reports that MRI has sensitivity 78% and specificity 92% for detecting complete pathological response (pCR) after neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy in rectal cancer. The reference standard is surgical pathology. The study includes 180 patients who all underwent surgery. In clinical practice, the proposed use is to identify patients with cCR who can avoid surgery. What is the primary applicability concern?

options.A: The sensitivity of 78% is inadequate for any clinical use
options.B: The reference standard (surgical pathology) is unavailable in patients who avoid surgery — the study population inherently differs from the target population (patients in whom surgery is being withheld), creating an indirectness/applicability concern
options.C: MRI should be compared to PET-CT as the reference standard, not surgical pathology
options.D: Specificity of 92% is too low for clinical use in this application

explanation.correct: This is a fundamental applicability problem in diagnostic accuracy studies for watch-and-wait decisions: the study enrolled patients who all underwent surgery, so their true response status (reference standard) is available. But the intended clinical application is to use MRI to identify patients who can safely avoid surgery — in which case, the reference standard (surgical pathology) would never be available. The study tests MRI performance in operated patients, but the clinical question concerns non-operated patients. Additionally, the spectrum of disease in operated patients may systematically differ from patients being considered for watch-and-wait (operated patients may have had more equivocal imaging findings, making MRI interpretation harder). This is an indirectness/applicability concern under QUADAS-2 Domain 1 (patient selection) and is a form of spectrum bias. MRCS and OnCoRe studies specifically address this by following patients managed with watch-and-wait.

wrong.A: Whether sensitivity of 78% is 'adequate' depends on the clinical context and consequences of a false negative (missed residual cancer) — this is a clinical judgment, not an absolute cut-off.
wrong.C: Surgical pathology is the ideal reference standard for pCR — it is not the source of the applicability concern.
wrong.D: Specificity of 92% means 8% false positives — patients incorrectly identified as not achieving pCR. In a watch-and-wait strategy, false negatives (missed residual disease) are more clinically critical than false positives.

explanation.guideline: QUADAS-2 applicability concerns — patient selection applicability: 'Are there concerns that the included patients and setting do not match the review question?'
explanation.trial: Habr-Gama A et al. Complete clinical response after neoadjuvant chemoradiation therapy for distal rectal cancer: characterisation of criteria. Ann Surg Oncol 2016.
explanation.pearl: Diagnostic studies for watch-and-wait decisions have an inherent verification problem: the reference standard (surgery) is withheld in the very patients the test is designed to identify. Studies must use surrogate outcomes (tumour regrowth, endoscopic biopsy, long-term survival) as reference standards — fundamentally different from operated populations.

questions.stem: A CHEERS 2022 appraisal of a surgical economic evaluation notes that the analysis takes a 'hospital perspective' rather than a 'healthcare payer perspective' or 'societal perspective.' What is the clinical significance of this perspective choice?

options.A: Perspective choice has no impact on the ICER — all perspectives produce the same result
options.B: A hospital perspective includes only direct hospital costs (theatre time, hospital stay, prosthetics) and excludes: outpatient follow-up costs, community care costs, patient productivity losses, and long-term medication costs — potentially underestimating the true economic impact and limiting generalisability of the ICER to resource allocation decisions
options.C: The hospital perspective is the gold standard for health economic evaluations — payer perspectives introduce bias
options.D: Hospital perspective evaluations are exempt from CHEERS reporting requirements

explanation.correct: The perspective of a health economic evaluation defines which costs and consequences are included in the analysis. A hospital perspective includes only costs that fall on the hospital: theatre time, operating materials, anaesthesia, hospital stay, re-operations, and inpatient complications. It excludes: outpatient follow-up appointments, community nursing costs, home rehabilitation, medications prescribed after discharge, patient productivity losses (time off work), caregiver costs, and emergency re-admissions to other hospitals. For CRS+HIPEC, this would exclude: the costs of post-operative community care, long-term surveillance imaging, and secondary treatment costs for recurrence — all of which are substantial. Health technology appraisal bodies (NICE, AEMPS) require at minimum a healthcare payer perspective; many require societal perspective for conditions affecting working-age patients. The hospital perspective limits the generalisability of the ICER beyond the hospital's internal resource allocation context.

wrong.A: Perspective fundamentally determines which costs are included — a hospital perspective ICER and a societal perspective ICER for the same intervention will produce different values, sometimes dramatically so.
wrong.C: No single perspective is universally 'gold standard' — the appropriate perspective depends on the decision-making audience (hospital management vs national payer vs society).
wrong.D: CHEERS 2022 item 4 explicitly requires the perspective to be stated and justified — it applies regardless of which perspective is chosen.

explanation.guideline: CHEERS 2022 item 4 — 'Characterise the perspective of the study and why it was selected. Describe the populations that costs and consequences will be measured for'
explanation.trial: Drummond MF et al. Methods for the Economic Evaluation of Health Care Programmes. 4th ed. Oxford University Press 2015 — reference text for health economic methodology.
explanation.pearl: Perspective hierarchy: **Societal** (broadest — all costs regardless of who bears them) > **Healthcare payer** (NHS/INSALUD costs only) > **Hospital** (narrowest — inpatient costs only). For national guideline and formulary decisions, payer perspective is minimum; societal perspective preferred when productivity effects are large.

questions.stem: A published guideline on rectal cancer surgery was assessed using AGREE II by two independent appraisers. For Domain 6 (Editorial independence), one appraiser scores 92/100 and the other scores 18/100. What is the most appropriate approach to resolve this discrepancy?

options.A: Average the two scores — (92+18)/2 = 55/100 is the consensus score
options.B: The appraisers should discuss the discrepancy, examine the specific items rated differently, reach consensus or involve a third appraiser; systematic disagreement may indicate ambiguous guideline reporting on COI or funding
options.C: The higher score takes precedence — assume the guideline is of adequate quality unless proven otherwise
options.D: The lower score takes precedence — critical appraisal should be conservative

explanation.correct: AGREE II methodology specifies that 2–4 appraisers independently score each item (scored 1–7), and discrepancies should be resolved through discussion, consensus, or involvement of a third appraiser. A large discrepancy (92 vs 18/100 = 74-point difference) on Domain 6 suggests that the appraisers interpreted the guideline's reporting of editorial independence very differently. Domain 6 items cover: whether funding body had influence on guideline content (item 22) and whether competing interests of development group members were recorded and addressed (item 23). The discrepancy may reflect: one appraiser found explicit COI declarations while the other did not (reporting ambiguity); different interpretation of what constitutes adequate COI disclosure; or one appraiser accessed supplementary material (COI forms) not reviewed by the other. Resolution requires item-by-item comparison of scoring and explicit discussion.

wrong.A: Averaging without discussion is not recommended by AGREE II — it produces a score that does not reflect either appraiser's evidence-based assessment.
wrong.C: Defaulting to the higher score without investigation ignores the possibility that the lower score reflects genuine quality concerns — confirmation bias in guideline acceptance.
wrong.D: Defaulting to the lower score without investigation may be excessively conservative — the discrepancy may reflect an information asymmetry rather than genuine quality failure.

explanation.guideline: AGREE II User Manual — 'Discrepancies should be discussed and resolved; where consensus cannot be reached, a third party should adjudicate'
explanation.trial: Brouwers MC et al. AGREE II: advancing guideline development, reporting and evaluation in health care. CMAJ 2010.
explanation.pearl: AGREE II Domain 6 (Editorial independence) is frequently problematic in surgical guideline appraisal because: (1) many surgical guidelines are developed by professional societies with industry partnerships; (2) COI declarations are often buried in supplementary material or disclosed only for individual recommendations; (3) the distinction between industry funding of the society vs the guideline development process itself is often unclear.

questions.stem: A retrospective cohort study using a national surgical database compares 30-day mortality after emergency colectomy in patients aged <75 vs ≥75 years. The older group has higher rates of all comorbidities, ASA class, and preoperative sepsis. After multivariable adjustment, age ≥75 remains independently associated with higher 30-day mortality (OR 2.3, 95%CI 1.8–2.9). Which statement best applies ROBINS-I confounding assessment to this finding?

options.A: Multivariable adjustment eliminates confounding — OR 2.3 represents the true causal effect of age
options.B: Multivariable adjustment reduces but does not eliminate confounding — residual confounding by unmeasured factors (frailty, social support, preoperative functional status, sarcopenia) may explain part of the observed association
options.C: The adjusted OR is invalid — only propensity score analysis is acceptable for confounding control in observational studies
options.D: ROBINS-I is not applicable to database studies — only to prospective cohort studies

explanation.correct: ROBINS-I Domain 1 (Confounding) assesses whether all important confounders were measured and adequately adjusted. Multivariable adjustment controls for confounders that were measured and included in the model — but cannot account for unmeasured confounders. In the age-mortality comparison after emergency colectomy, key unmeasured confounders include: frailty index (a composite measure of vulnerability not captured by ASA or comorbidity count); preoperative functional status (ADL/IADL); nutritional status and sarcopenia (not routinely coded in databases); social support and post-discharge care capacity; patient and family preferences for intensive care escalation. These factors are systematically associated with both age and mortality — omitting them from the model means residual confounding inflates the estimated OR. ROBINS-I would rate this as 'Moderate' or 'Serious' confounding risk if these factors are not captured.

wrong.A: Multivariable adjustment cannot eliminate confounding by unmeasured variables — this is the fundamental limitation of observational data that randomisation addresses.
wrong.C: Propensity score analysis and multivariable regression are complementary methods with overlapping strengths and limitations — neither is universally superior, and neither eliminates unmeasured confounding.
wrong.D: ROBINS-I applies to all non-randomised studies of interventions, including database studies, prospective cohorts, case-control studies, and before-after studies.

explanation.guideline: Sterne JA et al. ROBINS-I, Domain 1: Confounding — 'Is there potential for residual confounding from variables that could not be adequately measured or controlled?'
explanation.trial: Bhatt DL, Mehta C. Adaptive designs for clinical trials. N Engl J Med 2016 — discusses the limits of observational confounding control.
explanation.pearl: The phrase 'independent predictor after adjustment' is commonly misunderstood as equivalent to 'causal factor.' It means: 'the association persisted after adjusting for the measured variables in our model.' Unmeasured confounders can still fully explain any 'independent' association. This is why RCTs are needed for causal claims.

questions.stem: An economic evaluation of PIPAC (pressurised intraperitoneal aerosol chemotherapy) for palliative treatment of colorectal peritoneal metastases reports an ICER of €8,500/QALY compared to best supportive care. A review panel notes that the analysis assumed equivalent overall survival between PIPAC and best supportive care (CMA approach). Which CHEERS 2022 concern does this raise?

options.A: Cost-minimisation analysis is inappropriate if there is uncertainty about whether outcomes are truly equivalent — if OS differs even slightly, CMA understates the ICER by ignoring outcome differences
options.B: PIPAC should be compared to HIPEC, not best supportive care
options.C: The ICER of €8,500 is too low to be credible — an error in the model is likely
options.D: Palliative interventions should not undergo health economic evaluation

explanation.correct: Cost-minimisation analysis (CMA) is only valid when outcomes are demonstrably equivalent between comparators — in which case, the cheapest option should be chosen. The assumption that PIPAC and best supportive care produce equivalent overall survival is unlikely to be robustly established for PIPAC, which is a relatively novel technique with limited RCT evidence. If PIPAC provides even a modest OS or QoL advantage over BSC, CMA is inappropriate — it would attribute the cost difference to the wrong outcome (none) when in reality there is an incremental benefit. CHEERS 2022 specifically requires the basis for a CMA assumption to be clearly stated and justified. Reviewers should ask: was the equivalence assumption based on an RCT head-to-head comparison? Or on indirect comparison of observational data? If the latter, uncertainty about equivalence is substantial and CEA or CUA should be used instead.

wrong.A: This is the correct answer — CMA is inappropriate when outcome equivalence is uncertain.
wrong.B: PIPAC vs best supportive care is a legitimate and clinically relevant comparison for palliative-intent analysis — HIPEC is for curative intent in resectable disease.
wrong.C: A low ICER for a palliative intervention compared to BSC is plausible, particularly if PIPAC can be delivered in outpatient settings with low procedural costs.
wrong.D: Palliative interventions absolutely should undergo economic evaluation — resource allocation decisions are needed in the palliative as well as curative setting.

explanation.guideline: CHEERS 2022 item 11 — 'State the rationale for, and limitations of, choosing the approach' including justification for CMA assumptions
explanation.trial: Alyami M et al. PIPAC applied to the unresectable peritoneal surface malignancies: outcomes and safety from a single-centre experience. Cancer Medicine 2019.
explanation.pearl: CMA applicability checklist: (1) Was equivalence demonstrated in a head-to-head RCT? (2) Does the CI around the outcome difference include only clinically negligible values? (3) Is biological plausibility supporting equivalence credible? If any answer is 'No' or 'Uncertain' — use CEA or CUA instead of CMA.

questions.stem: A reader critically appraises an RCT of antibiotic therapy vs appendicectomy for uncomplicated acute appendicitis. The trial reports a non-significant primary outcome (p=0.08) for treatment failure at 1 year, with OR 1.42 (95%CI 0.95–2.12). The paper concludes 'antibiotics are non-inferior to appendicectomy.' Which statistical concept has been incorrectly applied?

options.A: A p-value of 0.08 confirms non-inferiority — this is a legitimate interpretation
